questions.question: An inverted index maps:
options: document IDs to their full content
terms to the list of documents containing them
product IDs to DynamoDB partition keys
user queries to cached result sets
questions.explanation: An inverted index maps each term (word) to the list of documents (products) containing it — the core data structure enabling full-text search.

questions.question: You have a product page that is read 50,000 times/sec but updated once per hour. Which caching strategy gives the best read scalability?
options: Read-through cache with TTL matching update frequency
Write-through cache with synchronous invalidation
No cache — DynamoDB can handle 50K reads/sec natively
Cache only after the third read (lazy population with threshold)
questions.explanation: Read-through with TTL ~1h means nearly all reads hit the cache. Write-through adds synchronous write latency. DynamoDB can handle it but at much higher cost.

questions.question: Why is price NOT indexed in OpenSearch and instead served from a Redis cache with 5s TTL?
options: OpenSearch does not support numeric fields
Price changes ~100K times/sec; re-indexing on every change would overwhelm the search cluster
Redis is strongly consistent while OpenSearch is eventually consistent
DynamoDB cannot store floating-point numbers for prices
questions.explanation: Price updates at 100K/sec would require constant index writes. OpenSearch is optimized for reads, not high-frequency document mutations. Redis TTL 5s serves price reads cheaply while Aurora remains the source of truth.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a blue/green index alias in OpenSearch?
options: Route 50% of traffic to each index for A/B testing search algorithms
Build a new index in the background and atomically cut traffic over without downtime
Replicate the index across AWS regions for disaster recovery
Separate read and write operations to different index shards
questions.explanation: A blue/green alias swap lets you build a fresh index (green) while the old one (blue) serves live traffic. Once the new index is ready, you update the alias pointer atomically — zero downtime reindex.

questions.question: A new product has zero sales history. In Amazon's ranking system, what is the most appropriate initial ranking strategy?
options: Assign rank 0 (bottom) until it has 100 sales
Use category-level average signals as a prior and apply a new-product boost for temporary visibility
Immediately rank it above all existing products to guarantee exposure
Do not index new products until they have at least one review
questions.explanation: Cold-start products use category-level signals as a Bayesian prior. A temporary boost surfaces them to early buyers. Rank 0 guarantees failure; immediate top rank is unfair to established products.

questions.question: Pre-computed facet counts in Redis are refreshed every 5 minutes. A customer filters by 'Prime only' and sees a count of 1,240 items, but clicking through shows 1,238. Why?
options: Redis has a bug in its ZSET implementation
DynamoDB and OpenSearch are out of sync
The facet cache is slightly stale — counts reflect a 5-minute-old snapshot while the live index has changed
The customer's browser cached the old count
questions.explanation: Pre-computed facets trade strict accuracy for performance. A 2-item discrepancy over 5 minutes is acceptable and far cheaper than running live aggregations at 500K QPS.

questions.question: During Black Friday, 10,000 product cache entries all expire at exactly midnight (TTL was set uniformly). What failure mode does this cause and how do you prevent it?
options: DynamoDB throttling — prevented by enabling DynamoDB Streams
Cache stampede — all servers simultaneously query the backend; prevented by jittered TTLs and probabilistic early expiration
OpenSearch index corruption — prevented by taking a snapshot before midnight
Redis OOM — prevented by increasing Redis memory before the event
questions.explanation: Uniform TTL expiry causes a thundering herd (cache stampede). Jittered TTLs spread expirations over a time window. Probabilistic early expiration re-warms the cache before actual expiry.

questions.question: You need to add full-text search in Japanese (which requires character-level tokenization, not space-based). What is the correct approach in OpenSearch?
options: Store Japanese products in a separate DynamoDB table with a different partition key
Use a locale-specific analyzer (e.g., Kuromoji for Japanese) configured per index or per field
Transliterate all Japanese text to romaji before indexing
OpenSearch does not support non-Latin character sets
questions.explanation: OpenSearch supports pluggable analyzers. Kuromoji is the standard Japanese morphological analyzer. Each locale needs its own analyzer — German needs a compound-word splitter, Arabic needs RTL handling, etc.
